planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall with the top third above the soil surface in well-drained, humus-rich soil in part to full shade; Veltheimia bracteata goes summer-dormant (June–August) and must be kept dry during this rest period — reduce watering significantly as foliage yellows and allow it to die back naturally before storage or dry pot rest. It resumes active growth in fall.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall in well-drained, humus-rich soil in full to part shade; forest lily is a South African bulb in the hyacinth family that thrives in the dappled shade conditions that defeat most flowering bulbs — producing handsome rosettes of large, glossy, wavy-edged leaves in autumn, then, in late winter and early spring (January–March), tall pinkish-mottled scapes topped with dense, drooping clusters of tubular flowers in warm rose-pink to coral-pink that closely resemble a soft-colored red-hot poker or kniphofia in miniature. The combination of shade tolerance, winter-to-spring bloom period, and attractive foliage through the cool season makes it uniquely valuable for shaded gardens in zones 9–11 where almost no other flowering bulb performs reliably in shade. After flowering it enters mandatory summer dormancy (June through August) and must be kept relatively dry during this period — a reversed dormancy cycle opposite to most northern temperate bulbs. Outstanding as a container specimen for a shaded patio and excellent for naturalizing under evergreen trees.

planting.note: Plant stem cuttings (3–4 node sections of the hollow stems) horizontally 2–3 inches deep in moist, fertile soil after last frost; tree dahlia's hollow, bamboo-like stems can be harvested in fall before frost, dried briefly, then cut into sections and planted like bamboo cuttings in spring. In zones 9–10 it is perennial above ground; in zone 8 it dies back to tuberous roots and resprouts.

directions: Plant stem cuttings in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; tree dahlia is the species dahlia from which all hybrid garden dahlias are partly derived — an enormous perennial of the Mexican highlands that grows 10–20 feet tall on stout, hollow, bamboo-like stems in a single season, then bursts into bloom in October through December with masses of large, 4–5 inch, lavender-pink single flowers with golden-yellow centers arranged along towering stems. The timing is critical and remarkable: tree dahlia is strictly day-length sensitive and will not initiate flowering until autumn days shorten in October, meaning it produces all of its spectacular display in the final weeks of the season, providing late color when everything else has finished. In zone 9 and frost-free areas it becomes a permanent perennial shrub, eventually developing thick, persistent stems from year to year. The hollow stems can be harvested, dried, and replanted as cuttings the following spring for easy propagation. An extraordinary statement plant for large gardens seeking drama at tropical scale in the fall.

planting.note: Plant small, scaly bulbs 2 inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil in spring after frost danger passes; iron cross oxalis goes dormant in winter and the bulbs can be left in the ground in zones 7–10. In colder zones, lift and store dry indoors. Bulbs multiply freely to form attractive clumps over the seasons.

directions: Plant scaly bulbs in spring in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; iron cross oxalis is one of the most cheerful and distinctive small bulbs available, producing four-parted, clover-like leaves in fresh green with a bold, chocolate-purple, cross-shaped zone in the center of each leaflet — the iron-cross pattern that gives it its common name and makes it instantly recognizable. The deep rose-pink to rose-red flowers appear in clusters above the foliage throughout summer, adding color from late spring until fall. A native of the Mexican highlands, it forms compact clumps that multiply steadily from the scaly bulblets and provide a weed-suppressing carpet through the warm season. The foliage folds closed in direct midday heat and at night (a characteristic of all oxalis), reopening in morning and evening light. In mild climates (zones 7–10) the bulbs overwinter in the ground to return dependably each spring. Excellent for container displays, rock gardens, and front-of-border edges where the boldly patterned foliage provides season-long textural interest.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or plant container-grown plants in consistently moist, humus-rich, acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in shade; this rhizomatous spreader naturalizes slowly to form a colony under trees. Keep well-watered through summer heat — wilting is a reliable drought indicator. Hardy to zone 7 with light mulch protection.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, humus-rich, acidic to neutral soil in full to part shade; Chinese hardy impatiens is one of the finest perennial groundcovers for deep shade, native to forested mountain slopes of Sichuan province in China and completely unlike the annual impatiens of traditional bedding plant use. Its dark, narrow, lance-shaped leaves carry a broad, silver-white central stripe along the midrib that brightens the shaded garden floor even in very low light — a near-luminous quality making it exceptional for animating dark corners under large trees. In autumn it produces small, hooded, pale-yellow flowers characteristic of wild impatiens, an attractive but modest secondary feature to the season-long foliage display. It spreads by rhizomes to form a dense, weed-suppressing carpet in shaded spaces where little else thrives, and is hardy to zone 7 with light winter mulch. Evergreen or semi-evergreen in mild winters. A highly refined, patterned alternative to hostas for the woodland garden, combining well with ferns, heucheras, and other shade perennials.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in late summer or plant container-grown plants in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to part shade; sweet viburnum grows 2–3 feet per year when established and can be maintained as a dense formal hedge by trimming 2–3 times per year, or allowed to develop into an informal screen or small tree reaching 15–20 feet. It is damaged at temperatures below 15°F.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to part shade; sweet viburnum is one of the most widely planted evergreen screening shrubs of the Gulf Coast, Deep South, and Florida — valued for its large, glossy, dark-green leaves, rapid growth rate, and the intensely fragrant white flower panicles produced in late March through May that fill the surrounding garden with a sweet, orange-blossom-like fragrance. After flowering, clusters of small fruits ripen from red to black and attract birds. The plant adapts equally well to full sun and part shade and grows rapidly to form a dense privacy screen or hedge 10–20 feet tall. It tolerates brief dips to 15°F but is most reliable from zone 8b southward. Shearing 2–3 times per year maintains it as a tight formal hedge; left unpruned it develops into a handsome large multi-stemmed shrub or small tree with layered, spreading branches. The cultivar 'Emerald Lustre' offers especially large, deeply glossed leaves. One of the fastest and most reliable evergreen privacy solutions for Gulf Coast and Florida landscapes.
